title: Geometry
blurb: From Pythagoras to sectors, similarity, and 3D volumes — the figures the exam always draws.

forms.title: The Pythagorean theorem
forms.skill: c² = a² + b² — hypotenuse from the legs.

variants.statement: A right triangle has legs $6$ and $8$. Find the hypotenuse.
A right triangle has legs $5$ and $12$. Find the hypotenuse.
A right triangle has legs $9$ and $12$. Find the hypotenuse.
A right triangle has legs $8$ and $15$. Find the hypotenuse.
A triangle has base $12$ and height $5$. Find its area.
variants.explanation: $A = \frac{1}{2}bh = \frac{1}{2} \cdot 12 \cdot 5 = 30$. Forgetting the $\frac12$ gives the surrounding rectangle instead.

variants.statement: A triangle has base $10$ and height $7$. Find its area.
variants.explanation: $A = \frac{1}{2}bh = \frac{1}{2} \cdot 10 \cdot 7 = 35$. Forgetting the $\frac12$ gives the surrounding rectangle instead.

variants.statement: A triangle has base $16$ and height $9$. Find its area.
variants.explanation: $A = \frac{1}{2}bh = \frac{1}{2} \cdot 16 \cdot 9 = 72$. Forgetting the $\frac12$ gives the surrounding rectangle instead.

variants.statement: A triangle has base $14$ and height $6$. Find its area.
variants.explanation: $A = \frac{1}{2}bh = \frac{1}{2} \cdot 14 \cdot 6 = 42$. Forgetting the $\frac12$ gives the surrounding rectangle instead.

forms.title: Circle area & circumference
forms.skill: A = πr² (fills), C = 2πr (wraps) — don't swap them.

variants.statement: A circle has radius $6$. Find its area, exactly.
variants.explanation: $A = \pi r^2 = \pi \cdot 6^2 = 36\pi$. ($2\pi r = 12\pi$ is the circumference — length, not area.)

variants.statement: A circle has radius $5$. Find its circumference, exactly.
variants.explanation: $C = 2\pi r = 10\pi$. ($\pi r^2 = 25\pi$ is the area.)

variants.statement: A circle has radius $9$. Find its area, exactly.
variants.explanation: $A = \pi r^2 = \pi \cdot 9^2 = 81\pi$. ($2\pi r = 18\pi$ is the circumference — length, not area.)

variants.statement: A circle has radius $7$. Find its circumference, exactly.
variants.explanation: $C = 2\pi r = 14\pi$. ($\pi r^2 = 49\pi$ is the area.)

variants.statement: A 45-45-90 triangle has hypotenuse $8$. Find each leg.
variants.explanation: Legs are hypotenuse over $\sqrt2$: $\frac{8}{\sqrt2} = \frac{8\sqrt2}{2} = 4 \sqrt{2}$.

variants.statement: A 30-60-90 triangle has hypotenuse $12$. Find the SHORT leg (opposite $30°$).
variants.explanation: The short leg is HALF the hypotenuse: $\frac{12}{2} = 6$. The $\sqrt3$ belongs to the long leg.

variants.statement: A 45-45-90 triangle has hypotenuse $10$. Find each leg.
variants.explanation: Legs are hypotenuse over $\sqrt2$: $\frac{10}{\sqrt2} = \frac{10\sqrt2}{2} = 5 \sqrt{2}$.

variants.statement: A 30-60-90 triangle has hypotenuse $18$. Find the SHORT leg (opposite $30°$).
variants.explanation: The short leg is HALF the hypotenuse: $\frac{18}{2} = 9$. The $\sqrt3$ belongs to the long leg.

variants.statement: Find the area of a $60°$ sector of a circle with radius $6$.
variants.explanation: A sector is a fraction of the disk: $\frac{60}{360} \cdot \pi \cdot 6^2 = \frac{1}{6} \cdot 36\pi = 6 \pi$.

variants.statement: Find the area of a $120°$ sector of a circle with radius $9$.
variants.explanation: A sector is a fraction of the disk: $\frac{120}{360} \cdot \pi \cdot 9^2 = \frac{1}{3} \cdot 81\pi = 27 \pi$.

variants.statement: Find the area of a $90°$ sector of a circle with radius $12$.
variants.explanation: A sector is a fraction of the disk: $\frac{90}{360} \cdot \pi \cdot 12^2 = \frac{1}{4} \cdot 144\pi = 36 \pi$.

variants.statement: Find the area of a $150°$ sector of a circle with radius $6$.
variants.explanation: A sector is a fraction of the disk: $\frac{150}{360} \cdot \pi \cdot 6^2 = \frac{5}{12} \cdot 36\pi = 15 \pi$.

forms.title: Similar triangles
forms.skill: Corresponding sides share one multiplier k — never an added amount.

variants.statement: Two similar triangles: the first has sides $4$ and $6$; the corresponding side to $4$ in the second is $10$. Find the side corresponding to $6$.
variants.explanation: Scale factor $k = \frac{10}{4} = \frac{5}{2}$. Corresponding sides scale together: $6 \cdot \frac{5}{2} = 15$. (Adding $6$ to everything is the classic wrong move — similarity multiplies, never adds.)

variants.statement: Two similar triangles: the first has sides $3$ and $8$; the corresponding side to $3$ in the second is $9$. Find the side corresponding to $8$.
variants.explanation: Scale factor $k = \frac{9}{3} = 3$. Corresponding sides scale together: $8 \cdot 3 = 24$. (Adding $6$ to everything is the classic wrong move — similarity multiplies, never adds.)

variants.statement: Two similar triangles: the first has sides $5$ and $7$; the corresponding side to $5$ in the second is $15$. Find the side corresponding to $7$.
variants.explanation: Scale factor $k = \frac{15}{5} = 3$. Corresponding sides scale together: $7 \cdot 3 = 21$. (Adding $10$ to everything is the classic wrong move — similarity multiplies, never adds.)

variants.statement: Two similar triangles: the first has sides $6$ and $10$; the corresponding side to $6$ in the second is $9$. Find the side corresponding to $10$.
variants.explanation: Scale factor $k = \frac{9}{6} = \frac{3}{2}$. Corresponding sides scale together: $10 \cdot \frac{3}{2} = 15$. (Adding $3$ to everything is the classic wrong move — similarity multiplies, never adds.)

forms.title: Volumes of solids
forms.skill: Cylinder πr²h; cone adds ⅓; sphere ⁴⁄₃πR³.

variants.statement: Find the volume of a cone with radius $3$ and height $4$, exactly.
variants.explanation: Cone: $V = \frac13 \pi r^2 h = \frac13 \pi \cdot 9 \cdot 4 = 12\pi$. The ⅓ is what separates it from the cylinder.

variants.statement: Find the volume of a cylinder with radius $5$ and height $6$, exactly.
variants.explanation: Cylinder: $V = \pi r^2 h = \pi \cdot 25 \cdot 6 = 150\pi$.

variants.statement: Find the volume of a sphere with radius $6$, exactly.
variants.explanation: Sphere: $V = \frac43 \pi R^3 = \frac43 \pi \cdot 216 = 288\pi$ — cube the radius, not square it.

variants.statement: Find the volume of a cone with radius $6$ and height $8$, exactly.
variants.explanation: Cone: $V = \frac13 \pi r^2 h = \frac13 \pi \cdot 36 \cdot 8 = 96\pi$. The ⅓ is what separates it from the cylinder.

forms.title: The space diagonal
forms.skill: d² = l² + w² + h² — all three dimensions enter.

variants.statement: Find the space diagonal of a $3 \times 4 \times 12$ box.
variants.explanation: $d = \sqrt{l^2 + w^2 + h^2} = \sqrt{9 + 16 + 144} = \sqrt{169} = 13$ — Pythagoras across the floor, then up the wall.

variants.statement: Find the space diagonal of a $2 \times 3 \times 6$ box.
variants.explanation: $d = \sqrt{l^2 + w^2 + h^2} = \sqrt{4 + 9 + 36} = \sqrt{49} = 7$ — Pythagoras across the floor, then up the wall.

variants.statement: Find the space diagonal of a $9 \times 12 \times 8$ box.
variants.explanation: $d = \sqrt{l^2 + w^2 + h^2} = \sqrt{81 + 144 + 64} = \sqrt{289} = 17$ — Pythagoras across the floor, then up the wall.

variants.statement: Find the space diagonal of a $2 \times 6 \times 9$ box.
variants.explanation: $d = \sqrt{l^2 + w^2 + h^2} = \sqrt{4 + 36 + 81} = \sqrt{121} = 11$ — Pythagoras across the floor, then up the wall.
